section.title: Classical vector-calculus identities
section.p: ended with two familiar-looking derivatives: \nabla\times \mathbf F \qquad\text{and}\qquad \nabla\cdot \mathbf F. In vector notation they look like separate operations. In form notation they are successive appearances of the same operation: d. That is why two famous identities are really one fact.

subsection.title: A gradient field has no local rotation
subsection.p: Suppose a metal plate has temperature T(x,y,z)=x^2y+yz+3z. The gradient field is \nabla T=(2xy,\ x^2+z,\ y+3). This field points in the direction of fastest temperature increase.

Compute its curl:
\nabla\times(\nabla T) \amp= \left( \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(y+3) - \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(x^2+z), \right. \amp\qquad \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(2xy) - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(y+3), \amp\qquad\left. \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x^2+z) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2xy) \right) \amp= (1-1,\ 0-0,\ 2x-2x) \amp= \mathbf 0.

The cancellations are not an accident. A gradient field comes from a potential function. It points uphill. A tiny paddle wheel placed in a pure uphill field may be pushed, but it has no first-order tendency to spin.

In the language of 1-forms, the potential T has differential dT=T_x\,dx+T_y\,dy+T_z\,dz. For our example, dT=2xy\,dx+(x^2+z)\,dy+(y+3)\,dz. Taking d again gives d(dT)=0. That is the form version of \nabla\times(\nabla T)=\mathbf 0.

proof.p:
Write 
abla f=(f_x,f_y,f_z). Then 
abla	imes(
abla f) = (f_{zy}-f_{yz},\ f_{xz}-f_{zx},\ f_{yx}-f_{xy}). If the second partial derivatives are continuous, then the mixed partials agree: f_{zy}=f_{yz},\qquad f_{xz}=f_{zx},\qquad f_{yx}=f_{xy}. So every component is zero.

subsection.p: The hypothesis about continuous second partial derivatives is doing real work.

statement.p: Define f(x,y)= \begin{cases} \dfrac{xy(x^2-y^2)}{x^2+y^2}, \amp (x,y)\ne(0,0),\\[2mm] 0, \amp (x,y)=(0,0). \end{cases} This function has second mixed partial derivatives at the origin, but they are not equal.

First compute f_x(0,y). For y\ne0,
f_x(0,y) \amp= \lim_{h\to0} \frac{f(h,y)-f(0,y)}{h} \amp= \lim_{h\to0} \frac{hy(h^2-y^2)}{h^2+y^2}\cdot\frac1h \amp= \lim_{h\to0} y\frac{h^2-y^2}{h^2+y^2} \amp= -y.
Also f_x(0,0)=0. Therefore f_{xy}(0,0) = \lim_{y\to0}\frac{f_x(0,y)-f_x(0,0)}{y} = \lim_{y\to0}\frac{-y}{y} = -1.

Now compute f_y(x,0). For x\ne0,
f_y(x,0) \amp= \lim_{h\to0} \frac{f(x,h)-f(x,0)}{h} \amp= \lim_{h\to0} \frac{xh(x^2-h^2)}{x^2+h^2}\cdot\frac1h \amp= \lim_{h\to0} x\frac{x^2-h^2}{x^2+h^2} \amp= x.
Also f_y(0,0)=0. Therefore f_{yx}(0,0) = \lim_{x\to0}\frac{f_y(x,0)-f_y(0,0)}{x} = \lim_{x\to0}\frac{x}{x} = 1.

So  oxed{ f_{xy}(0,0)=-1, \qquad f_{yx}(0,0)=1. } If we form the field 
abla f=(f_x,f_y,0), then at the origin the z-component of 
abla	imes(
abla f) would be f_{yx}(0,0)-f_{xy}(0,0)=2. The identity failed because the second partial derivatives were not continuous enough to force mixed partials to agree.

subsection.p: The moral is small but sharp: the identity \nabla\times(\nabla f)=\mathbf 0 belongs to sufficiently smooth functions. It is not a purely formal trick with symbols.

subsection.title: A curl field has no source strength
subsection.p: Now look at a curl instead of a gradient.

Let \mathbf F(x,y,z)=(0,xz,0). Then
\nabla\times\mathbf F \amp= \left( 0-\frac{\partial}{\partial z}(xz), \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(0)-0, \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(xz)-0 \right) \amp= (-x,0,z).
Now take its divergence:
\nabla\cdot(\nabla\times\mathbf F) \amp= \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-x) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(0) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(z) \amp= -1+0+1 \amp= 0.

A curl field represents local spinning. Spinning can move material around, but by itself it does not create source strength. In form language, if \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz is the work form of \mathbf F, then d\omega is the flux form of 
abla	imes\mathbf F. Taking d again gives d(d\omega)=0. That is the form version of 
abla\cdot(
abla	imes\mathbf F)=0.

statement.p: Let \mathbf F=(P,Q,R) have continuous second partial derivatives on an open region. Then \boxed{ \nabla\cdot(\nabla\times\mathbf F)=0. } Equivalently, if \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz, then \boxed{ d(d\omega)=0. }

proof.p:
The curl is 
abla	imes\mathbf F = (R_y-Q_z,\ P_z-R_x,\ Q_x-P_y). Its divergence is

abla\cdot(
abla	imes\mathbf F) \amp= (R_y-Q_z)_x + (P_z-R_x)_y + (Q_x-P_y)_z \amp= R_{yx}-Q_{zx}+P_{zy}-R_{xy}+Q_{xz}-P_{yz}.
Regroup: (R_{yx}-R_{xy}) + (P_{zy}-P_{yz}) + (Q_{xz}-Q_{zx}). Each pair cancels because the second partial derivatives are continuous. Therefore 
abla\cdot(
abla	imes\mathbf F)=0.

subsection.p: The same smoothness warning appears again.

example.title: A nonsmooth vector potential can break the cancellation

statement.p:
Use the function from the previous example: f(x,y)=  egin{cases} \dfrac{xy(x^2-y^2)}{x^2+y^2}, \amp (x,y)
e(0,0),\[2mm] 0, \amp (x,y)=(0,0). \end{cases} Define a vector field in space by \mathbf A(x,y,z)=(0,0,f(x,y)). Then 
abla	imes\mathbf A=(f_y,-f_x,0). At the origin,

abla\cdot(
abla	imes\mathbf A) \amp= (f_y)_x+(-f_x)_y \amp= f_{yx}-f_{xy} \amp= 1-(-1) \amp= 2.
So the identity 
abla\cdot(
abla	imes\mathbf A)=0 fails at the origin. The formal cancellation depends on the mixed partials being allowed to trade order.

subsection.title: The single fact: d^2=0
subsection.p: The two identities now have the same explanation.
For a function f, df is a 1-form. Its exterior derivative is d(df). The identity d(df)=0 is the statement \nabla\times(\nabla f)=\mathbf 0.

For a work 1-form \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz, the derivative d\omega is the flux 2-form of the curl: d\omega=\Phi_{
abla	imes\mathbf F}. The identity d(d\omega)=0 is the statement 
abla\cdot(
abla	imes\mathbf F)=0.
So the two classical identities are one rule: \boxed{ d^2=0. }

A tiny piece of the algebra shows why. In two variables, df=f_x\,dx+f_y\,dy. Then
d(df) \amp= d(f_x)\wedge dx+d(f_y)\wedge dy \amp= (f_{xx}\,dx+f_{xy}\,dy)\wedge dx + (f_{yx}\,dx+f_{yy}\,dy)\wedge dy.
Now dx\wedge dx=0, \qquad dy\wedge dy=0, and dy\wedge dx=-dx\wedge dy. So
d(df) \amp= f_{xy}\,dy\wedge dx+f_{yx}\,dx\wedge dy \amp= (f_{yx}-f_{xy})\,dx\wedge dy.
When the mixed partials agree, this is 0.
The wedge product supplies the sign. Equality of mixed partials supplies the cancellation.

subsection.title: Potentials and topology warnings
subsection.p: The identity \nabla\times(\nabla f)=\mathbf 0 says every gradient field has zero curl.

The converse is more delicate. If \nabla\times\mathbf F=\mathbf 0, then \mathbf F often has a potential function locally. On a simple region with no holes, it may have a global potential: \mathbf F=\nabla f. But holes can prevent a global potential.

Recall the field \mathbf A(x,y,z) = \left( -\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}, \frac{x}{x^2+y^2}, 0 \right), defined away from the z-axis. A direct calculation gives \nabla\times\mathbf A=\mathbf 0 where the field is defined.

But around the unit circle \mathbf r(t)=(\cos t,\sin t,0), \qquad 0\le t\le2\pi, we get \mathbf A(\mathbf r(t))=(-\sin t,\cos t,0), and \mathbf r'(t)=(-\sin t,\cos t,0). Thus
\oint_C \mathbf A\cdot d\mathbf r \amp= \int_0^{2\pi} (-\sin t,\cos t,0)\cdot(-\sin t,\cos t,0)\,dt \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}1\,dt \amp= 2\pi.
If \mathbf A were a global gradient field on its whole domain, every closed-loop integral would be 0. This nonzero integral proves that no such global potential exists.

A similar warning appears one level higher. The identity 
abla\cdot(
abla	imes\mathbf A)=0 says every curl field has zero divergence. The converse asks whether every divergence-free field can be written as a curl: \mathbf F=
abla	imes\mathbf A. Again, topology can interfere.

Consider \mathbf G(x,y,z) =  rac{(x,y,z)}{(x^2+y^2+z^2)^{3/2}}, defined on \mathbb R^3\setminus\{(0,0,0)\}. Away from the origin, 
abla\cdot\mathbf G=0. But the outward flux through any sphere centered at the origin is 4\pi. If \mathbf G=
abla	imes\mathbf A for a smooth vector field \mathbf A defined on the punctured space, then the flux of \mathbf G through the sphere would be the flux of a curl through a closed surface. Stokes' theorem would give \iint_S(
abla	imes\mathbf A)\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS = \int_{\partial S}\mathbf A\cdot d\mathbf r = 0, because a sphere has no boundary. But the flux is 4\pi. So no global vector potential exists on the punctured space.
These warnings are not exceptions to the identities. They say something more subtle: local derivative tests do not always decide global questions. Smoothness and domain shape both matter.
That is why the final section of the chapter collects the traps. The formulas are short, but the surface, the orientation, the boundary, and the domain decide whether the formula may be used.
